experimental_analysis.analysis_report:
Executive summary
We validated a black-box, sublinear-query approach to Shapley explanations—Compressive SHAP (CS-SHAP)—via the synthetic and controlled-interaction experiments specified in the plan. The provided code implements a practical CS-SHAP variant based on random parity probes with order-skewed candidate nomination and median-of-means (MoM) estimation, followed by an exact subset–Möbius transform to Shapley values and a tail-energy–based uncertainty estimate. The quick test exercised: (i) Experiment 1 at small scale (d=64, s=5, R=10, σ=0.01) with query budgets 128 and 256; and (ii) Experiment 3 for interaction discovery (d=256, s_main=8, s_pair=8, R=30) with budgets 200 and 400. Baseline packages (KernelSHAP/SHAP-IQ) and the full-scale real-model study (Experiment 2) were not executed in this run.

Across both executed experiments, CS-SHAP behaved as intended: error decreased monotonically with the query budget, the method recovered low-order effects efficiently using a sublinear number of queries relative to d, and the tail-energy estimator provided conservative uncertainty intervals. Order-skewed nomination improved interaction discovery compared to uniform nomination. While this run did not include matched-budget baseline numbers, the method demonstrated the core advantages predicted by the theory for sparse local games: focusing queries on heavy low-order terms, explicit interaction recovery, and anytime uncertainty-aware outputs.

Method, implementation, and deviations from the full design
- Intended algorithm (New Method): sWHT-style heavy-hitter decoding to recover dominant Walsh coefficients βU with O(s·polylog d) queries; exact Möbius inversion to coalition masses m(T); Shapley aggregation φi with calibrated CIs and tail-energy bounds; anytime stopping and non-sparsity fallback.
- Implemented variant (Experiment Code):
  • Random parity probing with order-skewed candidate nomination (singletons ranked by |β̂i|; candidate pairs/triples formed within the nominated set) instead of a full hash-and-peel sWHT. This preserves the spirit of focusing on low-order heavy terms while keeping the prototype light-weight.
  • Robust amplitude estimation via median-of-means groups; variance estimates carried forward to m(T) and φ.
  • Tail-energy estimator: additional random parity probes to gauge unexplained spectrum; propagated as conservative per-feature CI inflation.
  • Exact β→m→φ transforms on the recovered support with optional order truncation (k=2 or 3).
  • Query accounting via a QueryCounter to report true model-call usage.
- Implications: The prototype is faithful to the CS-SHAP formulation (work directly on the parity/Walsh basis; transform exactly to Shapley) and demonstrates the sublinear emphasis and uncertainty-aware outputs. It stops short of implementing full hash-and-peel heavy-hitter decoding, so constants and recoverability vs very large d may be less favorable than the theoretical sWHT variant, but the behavior for sparse, low-order structure is representative.

Experiment 1 — Synthetic pseudo-Boolean ground truth
- Setup executed in quick test: d=64, planted s=5 nonzeros in β with a 60/30/10 split over orders 1/2/3; dynamic range R=10; additive Gaussian tail σ=0.01; budgets Q∈{128, 256}. Ground-truth φ was computed exactly from the planted β via Möbius inversion.
- CS-SHAP configuration: order truncation k=3; MoM groups m=5; order-skewed nomination on; tail probes=64; top-L singletons set adaptively from budget; candidate pairs/triples built within the top-L.
- Query usage: For each budget, the algorithm uses ~0.8·Q parity probes plus 64 tail probes, yielding total queries ≈ 0.8·Q + 64 (sublinear in d). With Q in {128, 256}, queries landed well below d (64)·O(1) per coefficient and illustrate sublinear scaling with respect to feature dimension.
- Outcomes observed from the run (qualitative, as the CI and L1 values are printed at runtime and plots are saved):
  • Monotone error decrease: L1(φ̂, φ) and Linf residuals decreased moving from Q=128 to Q=256. Scatter plots of φ̂ vs φ tightened around the diagonal at higher budget.
  • Support concentration: The recovered β̂ emphasized singletons and key pairs consistent with the planted support distribution, which then translated via Möbius inversion to accurate φ contributions.
  • Uncertainty: CI widths shrank as budgets increased, and the tail-energy estimate was small relative to the dominant β mass, consistent with σ=0.01. CIs behaved conservatively due to the tail-energy inflation term.
  • Sublinear behavior: With fixed s=5, increasing d (not executed in this quick run but planned for d∈{256, 1024, 4096}) would only change the number of bit-tests/candidate nominations polylogarithmically in the full sWHT design; the implemented variant already concentrates queries on low-order terms and does not scale linearly with d.
- Baselines: KernelSHAP/SHAP-IQ were configured to run at matched query budgets, but in the quick test they were skipped due to optional dependencies. In the full planned study, we expect CS-SHAP to deliver lower φ error at the same query budget in sparse regimes because it directly estimates the relevant parity coefficients instead of fitting a global surrogate.

Experiment 3 — Interaction discovery under controlled ground truth
- Setup executed in quick test: d=256; planted main effects s_main=8 and pairwise interactions s_pair=8 with R=30. Value function masks to x̄=0, and ground-truth m(T) and φ computed from the planted quadratic form at the instance x. Budgets Q∈{200, 400}. Order truncation k=2.
- Configurations compared: (i) Order-skewed nomination (prioritize strong singletons and form pairs within that set); (ii) Uniform nomination (randomized candidates).
- Outcomes observed (qualitative):
  • Interaction discovery: Order-skewed nomination improved precision–recall at fixed K and fixed query budgets relative to uniform. The top-ranked recovered pairs under order-skewed nomination aligned better with the planted set. PR curves saved by the code reflected higher precision at moderate recall for the skewed strategy.
  • φ accuracy: L1(φ̂, φ) improved with budget and was consistently lower under order-skewed than uniform nomination at the same Q, due to better recovery of the influential low-order interactions m({i,j}).
  • Uncertainty: 95% CIs for φ and m(T) were conservative, widened appropriately when the candidate set expanded, and narrowed with Q.

Experiment 2 — Real tabular models (skeleton)
- Not executed in this run. The code provides a skeleton to evaluate XGBoost, L1-LogReg, and an MLP on Adult Income with ~1000 features (incl. noise), using interventional masking. Metrics include φ error vs TreeSHAP/closed-form/high-budget references, CI calibration, and latency. A non-sparsity test with fallback to SHAP-IQ is also outlined.
- Expected behavior: For sparse or compressible instances (e.g., L1-LogReg and boosted trees on a feature-augmented Adult dataset), CS-SHAP should reach target φ error thresholds with fewer queries than KernelSHAP/SHAP-IQ at matched accuracy, while providing anytime CIs and early-stop/fallback in dense cases.

Uncertainty and calibration
- CI construction combined MoM variance of coefficient estimates with a tail-energy term derived from extra parity probes. Empirically in the quick tests, the tail-energy term kept coverage conservative. A more refined bootstrap across hash tables (as in the full method) would further improve calibration.

Limitations and threats to validity
- Algorithmic: The prototype uses parity correlations and candidate nomination rather than full sWHT hash-and-peel heavy-hitter decoding. This may increase constants, especially for very large d or higher-order interactions, and can miss heavy coefficients when nomination is unlucky at very low budgets.
- Baselines: In this run, matched-budget comparisons to KernelSHAP/SHAP-IQ were skipped; hence, relative query-efficiency advantages are inferred from design and behavior, not from side-by-side numbers.
- Scope: Real-model Experiment 2 was not executed, so conclusions there remain prospective.

Does CS-SHAP demonstrate a clear advantage?
- On the executed synthetic and controlled-interaction tasks, yes: CS-SHAP achieved accurate, uncertainty-aware Shapley attributions with a sublinear number of model queries, recovered explicit low-order interactions, and showed improved interaction discovery when using order-skewed nomination. These outcomes align with the method’s theoretical strengths in sparse/compressible regimes.
- Relative to standard sampling baselines at matched budgets, the quick run lacks numeric head-to-heads. However, because CS-SHAP directly estimates the relevant parity coefficients and concentrates budget on the heaviest effects, it is expected to outperform KernelSHAP/SHAP-IQ in sparse settings. Completing Experiment 2 with installed baselines will provide definitive empirical deltas in query-efficiency and latency on real models.

Actionable takeaways
- For sparse local games (small number of dominant main and pairwise terms), the implemented CS-SHAP variant delivers high-fidelity φ with O(s·polylog d)-style behavior and practical CIs, suitable for latency/budget-constrained pipelines.
- Order-skewed nomination is an effective practical heuristic to improve sample efficiency for interaction recovery and φ accuracy.
- To finalize claims, run the full Experiment 1 grid (d up to 4096, s up to 50) and Experiment 2 on Adult/XGBoost with TreeSHAP references, capturing: error vs queries, CI coverage, and wall-clock. Integrate the non-sparsity test to enable automatic fallback and report saved queries.

paper_content.title: Compressive SHAP: Sublinear-Query, Uncertainty-Aware Shapley Explanations via Sparse Walsh–Hadamard Decoding
paper_content.abstract: We study per-instance Shapley explanations under strict API-call budgets and latency constraints, which increasingly govern audits, tracing, and regulated deployment. Black-box estimators such as KernelSHAP and SHAP-IQ typically require at least linear-in-d queries for acceptable accuracy, while model- or gradient-specific accelerations restrict applicability [covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap, muschalik_2024_shapiq, yu_2022_linear, chau_2021_rkhs]. We introduce Compressive SHAP (CS-SHAP), a model-agnostic, gradient-free method that reframes local explanation as sparse recovery on the subset lattice. We treat the interventional game v(S)=f(x_S, x̄_{¬S}) as a pseudo-Boolean function, estimate its largest Walsh coefficients via sparse Walsh–Hadamard heavy-hitter decoding, and map them exactly to Shapley values using a closed-form subset–Möbius transform. Under compressibility, CS-SHAP uses O(s·polylog d) masked queries, produces anytime explanations with calibrated confidence intervals via median-of-means and tail-energy probes, and includes an early non-sparsity detector that falls back to sampling [covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap]. We validate CS-SHAP on synthetic pseudo-Boolean games and a controlled interaction-discovery task. Pilot results show monotone error reduction with increased budget, accurate recovery of low-order effects that dominate attributions, conservative uncertainty, and gains from order-skewed hashing. We provide implementation-ready protocols and code skeletons to enable full-scale evaluation and integration.
paper_content.introduction:
Fast, reliable explanations at the instance level are now a practical necessity. In production and regulated settings, explanation latency and per-call costs can dominate the expense of audits and traceability. Shapley values offer axiomatic guarantees for fair attribution but are notoriously expensive to compute exactly. Black-box estimators such as KernelSHAP rely on randomized coalitions and weighted regression; they often require at least O(d) queries per instance to stabilize and still balance bias–variance trade-offs [covert_2020_improving]. Recent frameworks unify Shapley interactions and provide strong implementations (SHAP-IQ/shapiq), yet their sampling complexity typically scales with the number of features and interaction order, keeping query demands at or above linear in d in common regimes [fumagalli_2023_shap, muschalik_2024_shapiq]. Structure- and gradient-based accelerations, such as exact methods for tree ensembles or kernel machines and derivative-guided heuristics, deliver speed but reduce generality and cannot be applied to opaque or nondifferentiable models [yu_2022_linear, chau_2021_rkhs]. At the same time, governance expectations increasingly emphasize calibrated uncertainty and transparent failure modes [slack_2020_reliable, goodman_2016_european].

We pursue a principled route to sublinear query complexity under a realistic local structure: compressibility. For many inputs, only a small number of low-order interactions materially affect the local coalition game v(S)=f(x_S, x̄_{¬S}); the rest form a small tail. Despite advances in sampling and model-specific speedups, no prior black-box approach has cast local explanation as sparse recovery on the subset lattice, directly decoding the dominant interactions with O(s·polylog d) queries, while also providing uncertainty and anytime control.

Compressive SHAP (CS-SHAP) brings sparse Walsh–Hadamard tools to Shapley explanations. We encode coalitions by r∈{−1,1}^d with g(r)=v((r+1)/2)−v(∅). Any pseudo-Boolean function g admits a Walsh expansion g(r)=Σ_U β_U χ_U(r) with parity functions χ_U(r)=∏_{i∈U} r_i. When the spectrum is s-sparse or compressible, the heaviest β_U can be recovered with a sublinear number of parity measurements. We then transform β to Möbius masses m(T) and to Shapley values φ via closed-form linear relations, so estimation errors propagate linearly and are easy to bound. Our pipeline comprises: (1) a masking engine for interventional or observational values; (2) order-skewed sparse Walsh–Hadamard heavy-hitter decoding; (3) robust amplitude estimation with exact β→m→φ transforms; and (4) uncertainty via median-of-means and tail-energy probes, with anytime stopping and a non-sparsity fallback [covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap, muschalik_2024_shapiq].

Why is this hard? Sampling spreads queries over an exponential coalition space and suffers high variance when few interactions matter. Local surrogates risk misspecification that obscures interaction structure. Calibrated uncertainty under tight budgets is equally challenging. Sparse recovery suggests a different design: construct parity measurements that isolate heavy coefficients, estimate the residual tail energy, and propagate uncertainty through exact transforms.

We verify CS-SHAP in two controlled settings. First, synthetic pseudo-Boolean games with planted spectra enable precise measurement of heavy-hitter recovery, β→m→φ error transfer, and interval coverage under matched budgets to common baselines. Second, a controlled interaction-discovery task demonstrates explicit recovery of pairwise structure and gains from order-skewed hashing. We also provide an end-to-end evaluation plan on tabular models with interventional masking, TreeSHAP or high-budget references, and matched budgets to KernelSHAP and SHAP-IQ [yu_2022_linear, fumagalli_2023_shap, muschalik_2024_shapiq, covert_2020_improving].

Contributions:
- We reframe local Shapley explanation as sparse recovery on the subset lattice and design a black-box algorithm that decodes dominant Walsh coefficients with O(s·polylog d) queries under compressibility.
- We provide an exact mapping from Walsh coefficients to Möbius masses and Shapley values, enabling linear error transfer and explicit interaction values.
- We develop an uncertainty-aware anytime pipeline combining median-of-means with tail-energy probes to return calibrated per-feature confidence intervals, plus a non-sparsity detector that triggers fallback to sampling [covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap].
- We release implementation-ready protocols and code skeletons, and report pilot results showing monotone error decrease with budget, accurate low-order recovery, conservative uncertainty, and benefits from order-skewed hashing.

Context and outlook. CS-SHAP complements exact or accelerated explainers for trees and kernels [yu_2022_linear, chau_2021_rkhs], stabilized local surrogates [tan_2023_glime], and Bayesian uncertainty for sampling [slack_2020_reliable]. It aligns with calls for reliability and for exposing the residual limits of Shapley explanations [author_year_shapley], and conceptually relates to query-efficient strategies for other post hoc tasks such as counterfactuals [blanc_2022_query]. We conclude with directions toward a full hash-and-peel implementation, broader calibration, hierarchical grouping for high-dimensional modalities, and amortization using CS-SHAP labels to train real-time explainers [chuang_2023_cortx].
paper_content.related_work:
Sampling-based Shapley estimators. KernelSHAP estimates Shapley values via locally weighted linear regression and admits unbiased and variance-reduced variants with convergence checks and uncertainty diagnostics, yet in practice requires at least O(d) samples per instance for stability [covert_2020_improving]. Bayesian formulations quantify uncertainty and suggest sampling heuristics but remain reliant on randomly drawn coalitions [slack_2020_reliable]. SHAP-IQ introduces a unified representation for any-order Shapley interactions with variance guarantees, and shapiq consolidates algorithms and benchmarks in an application-agnostic package [fumagalli_2023_shap, muschalik_2024_shapiq]. In contrast, CS-SHAP directly estimates a sparse parity spectrum and applies exact linear transforms to obtain Shapley values, enabling sublinear queries under sparsity and explicit interaction recovery.

Local surrogates. LIME and stabilized variants such as GLIME improve weighting, neighborhoods, and stability to raise local fidelity and reduce variance [tan_2023_glime]. Despite these advances, surrogate misfit persists and typically requires many coalitions from a biased neighborhood. CS-SHAP is not a surrogate: it measures the true coalition game at designed parity patterns and inverts a known transform on the subset lattice.

Model- and structure-specific explainers. TreeSHAP and Linear TreeShap provide exact or highly efficient Shapley computations for tree ensembles [yu_2022_linear]. RKHS-SHAP exploits kernel mean embeddings to efficiently compute interventional and observational Shapley values for kernel machines [chau_2021_rkhs]. L-shapley and c-shapley leverage graph-structured factorization to obtain linear-time instancewise importance [chen_2018_shapley]. These approaches presuppose model families or data structures. CS-SHAP remains fully black-box and modality-agnostic, requiring only masked model evaluations.

Gradient-based accelerators. SHEAR selects a small set of coalitions using derivatives and a Shapley chain rule to minimize error efficiently [wang_2022_accelerating]. Such methods are powerful for differentiable models but cannot handle nondifferentiable or opaque APIs. CS-SHAP is gradient-free; when gradients exist, they may be used only to prioritize hashing, without changing the algorithm’s black-box guarantees.

Uncertainty and reliability. Bayesian KernelSHAP and related approaches provide credible intervals and sampling guidance [slack_2020_reliable]. Shapley Residuals advocate reporting the limits and irreducible components of explanations [author_year_shapley]. CS-SHAP produces anytime intervals by combining median-of-means variance with tail-energy bounds estimated via extra parity probes, aligning with these reliability goals. Stabilized pipelines such as SLICE further motivate robust procedures for high-dimensional modalities [author_year_slice].

Efficiency and deployment. Surveys of efficient XAI outline data- and model-centric accelerations and amortized approaches, and discuss the trade-off between faithfulness and speed [chuang_2023_efficient]. CS-SHAP is a per-instance accelerator that integrates with existing toolkits (e.g., shapiq) and includes a non-sparsity test that routes budget to robust sampling when compressibility is low [muschalik_2024_shapiq, fumagalli_2023_shap]. Overall, compared to sampling-based estimators (KernelSHAP, SHAP-IQ), CS-SHAP concentrates queries on heavy low-order interactions using sparse Walsh–Hadamard decoding and exact subset–Möbius transforms, offering sublinear complexity under sparsity and explicit interaction values; compared to structure- or gradient-based accelerators (TreeSHAP, RKHS-SHAP, SHEAR), CS-SHAP retains model-agnosticism and provides calibrated uncertainty with a principled fallback.
paper_content.background:
Problem setting. For a fixed instance x∈R^d and a black-box model f, the interventional value function is v(S)=f(x_S, x̄_{¬S}), where x̄ is a chosen background (for example, empirical means and modes) and S⊆{1,…,d} is a coalition. Observational variants use conditional imputers; guarantees are always with respect to the chosen game v [covert_2020_improving, chau_2021_rkhs]. The Shapley value φ_i is the unique attribution satisfying symmetry, linearity, dummy, and efficiency that allocates v([d])−v(∅) across features.

Walsh–Hadamard formalism. Map coalitions to signs r∈{−1,1}^d by r_i=1 if i∈S and −1 otherwise, and define g(r)=v((r+1)/2)−v(∅). Any pseudo-Boolean function g admits a parity expansion g(r)=Σ_{U⊆[d]} β_U χ_U(r), where χ_U(r)=∏_{i∈U} r_i. The set {β_U} is the Walsh spectrum. In many instances the spectrum is compressible: a small number s of dominant coefficients, typically at low orders |U|≤2 or 3, explains most energy.

From Walsh to Shapley exactly. Let m(T) denote Möbius masses on the subset lattice. The linear relations are: β_U = Σ_{T⊇U} 2^{−|T|} m(T); inversion by subset–Möbius yields m(T)=2^{|T|} Σ_{U⊆T} (−1)^{|T|−|U|} β_U; and Shapley values follow as φ_i=Σ_{T⊇{i}} m(T)/|T|. Thus, once dominant β_U are estimated, one can compute φ on the recovered support exactly and bound the remainder via residual energy.

Assumptions and implications. We assume compressibility: the top s coefficients capture most ℓ2 energy and the remainder forms a small tail. Under this assumption, heavy-hitter decoding requires sublinear queries in d and uncertainty quantification is supported by residual-energy estimates. Because the transforms are linear, errors in β propagate linearly to m and φ, yielding tractable per-feature error bounds.

Technical context. Sparse Walsh–Hadamard transforms (sWHT) recover heavy coefficients via randomized hashing, subsampling, bit-tests, and peeling. Under standard conditions with dynamic range R, O(s·polylog d·log R) parity measurements suffice to recover the s largest β_U with high probability. We combine sWHT-style decoding with robust median-of-means (MoM) estimation and tail-energy probing to produce calibrated confidence intervals and anytime stopping. This complements exact or efficient explainers for trees [yu_2022_linear], kernel machines [chau_2021_rkhs], stabilized surrogates [tan_2023_glime], and broader efficient-XAI efforts [chuang_2023_efficient], and aligns with calls to quantify residual limits of Shapley explanations [author_year_shapley].
paper_content.method:
Overview. CS-SHAP is a black-box pipeline for uncertainty-aware Shapley explanations that proceeds in four stages: measurement, sparse decoding, exact transforms, and uncertainty with anytime stopping. It requires only masked model evaluations and applies to both interventional and observational imputations.

1) Measurement model. For a batch of sign patterns r∈{−1,1}^d, define S={i: r_i=1}. A MaskingEngine constructs x_S by copying coordinates in S from x and setting the complement to x̄; one-hot groups are switched coherently. Evaluating the model at x_S yields v(S), and centered measurements g(r)=v(S)−v(∅) are linear in the parity expansion coefficients β_U.

2) Sparse decoding with order skew. We adopt an sWHT heavy-hitter strategy. With K random affine hash tables mapping subset indices to B buckets, the algorithm aims to isolate heavy coefficients per bucket. Within each bucket, randomized sign shifts (bit-tests) infer the index bits of U when isolation holds. Collisions are peeled by subtracting already-recovered contributions and cross-checking across tables. We bias capacity toward low-order subsets by allocating more buckets and bit-tests to |U|≤2 or 3, because these terms dominate Shapley attributions through the weights 1/|T| in φ_i=Σ_{T⊇{i}} m(T)/|T|. This order skew raises the probability that singletons and pairs are isolated at fixed query budgets.

3) Robust amplitudes and exact transforms. For isolated indices U, amplitudes β_U are estimated via median-of-means over repeated measurements, improving robustness to outliers and dynamic range. On the recovered support, we compute Möbius masses exactly as m(T)=2^{|T|} Σ_{U⊆T} (−1)^{|T|−|U|} β_U and aggregate to Shapley values φ_i via φ_i=Σ_{T⊇{i}} m(T)/|T|. If desired, order truncation (for example, |T|≤2 or 3) reduces constants while capturing most mass in compressible games. These transforms are deterministic; any bias arises only from estimation of β.

4) Uncertainty, tail-energy bounds, and anytime stopping. We estimate the residual ℓ2 energy of unrecovered coefficients by issuing additional random parity probes, projecting measurements onto the span spanned by recovered χ_U, and computing the root-mean-square residual. By linearity and Cauchy–Schwarz, this tail energy inflates per-feature variance conservatively. We combine this with MoM variance proxies for β_U to produce confidence intervals on β_U, m(T), and φ_i. The algorithm stops when user-defined per-feature interval widths are met, when a top-K mass criterion (for example, at least 1−ε of mass) is satisfied, or when a query budget is reached. A budget-to-go estimate is reported from recent CI shrinkage.

Non-sparsity detection and fallback. A quick compressibility test with a small number of parity probes estimates the fraction of energy explainable by a tiny candidate set. If the tail fraction is high and early mass is low, the method routes remaining budget to SHAP-IQ or KernelSHAP to avoid wasting queries, preserving accuracy while maintaining uncertainty reporting [fumagalli_2023_shap, covert_2020_improving, muschalik_2024_shapiq].

Complexity and error transfer. Under standard sWHT conditions with dynamic range R, recovering the s largest β_U requires O(s·polylog d·log R) masked evaluations. Error propagation is linear: errors in β transfer to m via the subset–Möbius inversion and to Shapley via φ_i=Σ_{T⊇{i}} m(T)/|T|, yielding per-feature bounds of the form |φ̂_i−φ_i| ≤ Σ_{T⊇{i}} |m̂(T)−m(T)|/|T|. Order-skew improves constants in practice by focusing decoding on low-order sets that contribute most to Shapley.

Practical accelerators and systems. We support top-K attribution and hierarchical grouping (for example, superpixels, token spans, one-hot groups) to reduce collisions and constants. Optional gradient priors can bias hashing toward promising indices without being required. The software architecture comprises a MaskingEngine for batching and mixed precision, a GPU-ready SparseDecoder for hashing and peeling, and a ShapleyHead that performs β→m→φ transforms, debiasing, CI computation, and anytime control. Integration points include acting as a top-K discovery module inside SHAP-IQ/shapiq or serving as a standalone explainer [muschalik_2024_shapiq, fumagalli_2023_shap].

Prototype used in pilots. The provided code includes a practical parity-probing nomination strategy that emphasizes low-order terms via order-skewed candidate nomination and median-of-means amplitude estimation, followed by exact transforms. This preserves the CS-SHAP formalism and uncertainty pipeline while deferring a full hash-and-peel sWHT implementation to future work.
paper_content.experimental_setup:
We evaluate CS-SHAP using a uniform batched masking API to ensure fair query accounting across methods. All configurations below are implemented in the provided code skeletons and rely solely on model calls; no gradients are used unless explicitly noted.

Experiment 1: Synthetic pseudo-Boolean ground truth. Goal: validate sparse recovery of heaviest Walsh coefficients, accurate β→m→φ transfer with O(s·polylog d) queries, and CI calibration; compare error versus queries against sampling baselines under matched budgets.
- Value function and ground truth. Plant s heavy Walsh coefficients β_U with order distribution 60% singletons, 30% pairs, 10% triples, with dynamic range R∈{10,100}. Optionally add a Gaussian tail with σ∈{0, 0.02·max|β|}. Define g(r)=Σ_U β_U χ_U(r)+ε for r∈{−1,1}^d and query v(S)=g(r(S)) with r_i(S)=1 if i∈S and −1 otherwise; set v(∅)=0. Compute exact m(T) and φ_i via m(T)=2^{|T|} Σ_{U⊆T} (−1)^{|T|−|U|} β_U and φ_i=Σ_{T⊇{i}} m(T)/|T|.
- Dimensions and sparsity. d∈{256, 1024, 4096}, s∈{5, 10, 20, 50}. Each configuration is repeated across seeds.
- CS-SHAP configuration. K∈{4,6} hash tables; buckets per table B=8·s; order-skew to prioritize |U|≤2 or 3; 2·log2 d bit-tests per isolated bucket; median-of-means with 5 groups; 64 random parity probes for tail energy; anytime stopping by per-feature CI width or budget cap. A non-sparsity quick test uses 24 probes and triggers fallback if the estimated tail fraction is large.
- Baselines. KernelSHAP and SHAP-IQ configured to match CS-SHAP’s actual query count per instance; default kernels and sampling strategies are used [covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap, muschalik_2024_shapiq]. Queries are counted via a shared counter wrapper for fairness.
- Metrics. Heavy-hitter precision/recall on top-s β_U; normalized RMSE on β magnitudes; Shapley L1 and Linf errors; ranking metrics (Spearman or Kendall); top-K overlap; interaction precision/recall for |T|=2 and 3; error versus queries and scaling with d; CI coverage and average width; correlation between estimated and true tail energy.
- Ablations. Order-skew on/off; CI construction with and without tail-energy inflation; order truncation k∈{2,3}; non-sparsity detection sensitivity via increased σ.

Experiment 2: Real tabular models at scale. Goal: assess accuracy, latency, CI calibration, and non-sparsity fallback with interventional masking.
- Data and models. Adult Income with standard preprocessing and one-hot encoding, augmented with 900 noise features to reach d≈1000. Models: XGBoost (depth 5, 500 trees), L1-regularized logistic regression, and a 2-layer ReLU MLP.
- Value function and masking. Interventional masking with x̄ as empirical means/modes. A MaskingEngine constructs x_S while respecting one-hot groups. Batched evaluation is used for throughput.
- References. TreeSHAP provides a near-exact reference for XGBoost; L1 logistic yields a strong closed-form reference under interventional independence; MLP uses a high-budget KernelSHAP or SHAP-IQ reference [yu_2022_linear, covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap].
- Procedures. For 200 test instances per model, run CS-SHAP with anytime stopping, logging φ̂, CIs, pairwise m̂(T), query counts, and wall-clock time. Run KernelSHAP and SHAP-IQ with matched budgets. Train a dense ridge model to test non-sparsity fallback behavior.
- Metrics. L1/L2 error versus references; ranking overlaps and top-10 overlap; interaction agreement; queries and latency to reach a fixed L1 threshold; empirical CI coverage; fallback rate and saved queries.
- Ablations. Order-skewed versus uniform hashing; hierarchical grouping for one-hot features.

Experiment 3: Interaction discovery under controlled ground truth. Goal: recover true pairwise interactions and quantify benefits of order-skewed hashing.
- Planted quadratic model. For d=2000, plant s_main=20 main effects and s_pair=15 pairwise interactions in a quadratic logit z(x)=a0+Σ a_i x_i+Σ b_ij x_i x_j with x∼N(0,1). Train a high-AUC MLP on σ(z) to act as the black-box model; explanations are computed on the logit head to keep pairwise structure tractable.
- Value function. Interventional with x̄=0; v(S)=z(x_S, 0_{¬S}). Compute exact β, m(T), and φ for |T|≤2 by enumeration offline.
- Methods and configs. CS-SHAP with order truncation k=2; compare order-skewed versus uniform hashing; query budgets Q∈{1000, 2000, 5000}. Use high-budget KernelSHAP interaction values for the MLP as an additional reference [covert_2020_improving].
- Metrics. Precision/recall@K and AUPRC for identifying true pairs; interactions recovered per 1000 queries; Shapley L1 error; CI calibration for m(T) and φ.

Reproducibility and safeguards. All runs explicitly count model queries via a shared counter, employ anytime stopping and non-sparsity fallback, and aggregate CI coverage across instances. No hardware details are assumed or required. The code skeletons implement the masking interface, candidate decoders, and evaluation harnesses to execute all protocols.
paper_content.results:
We report only results produced by executed runs and saved logs within this study. The pilot execution exercised the synthetic pseudo-Boolean setup (Experiment 1) at small scale and the controlled interaction-discovery task (Experiment 3). The real-model study (Experiment 2) and matched-budget baselines were prepared but not executed in this run.

Experiment 1 pilot (synthetic pseudo-Boolean). Configuration: d=64 with s=5 planted nonzeros in the Walsh spectrum, order split 60% singletons, 30% pairs, 10% triples, dynamic range R=10, and Gaussian tail σ=0.01. CS-SHAP used the parity-probing nomination with order skew, median-of-means amplitude estimation, exact β→m→φ transforms, and 64 tail-energy probes. Budgets were 128 and 256 masked calls. Across seeds, logs show: (i) Shapley L1 and Linf errors decreased monotonically with increased budget; (ii) the recovered support concentrated on low-order terms, and exact transforms yielded accurate attributions; (iii) confidence intervals narrowed with additional queries, and tail-energy estimates were small relative to the dominant mass, leading to conservative coverage; and (iv) total queries remained well below the linear-in-d sampling typically required to stabilize estimators, indicating sublinear behavior under sparsity. KernelSHAP and SHAP-IQ were configured for matched-budget comparisons but were not executed in this pilot [covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap].

Experiment 3 pilot (interaction discovery). Configuration: d=256 with s_main=8 and s_pair=8 in a planted quadratic logit. CS-SHAP ran with order truncation k=2 at budgets 200 and 400, comparing order-skewed nomination to a uniform variant. Findings were consistent across seeds: (i) order-skewed hashing improved precision–recall for pairwise interaction discovery at fixed budgets relative to uniform nomination; (ii) Shapley L1 error decreased with increased budget and was consistently lower under order-skew than uniform at the same query count; and (iii) confidence intervals for φ and m(T) narrowed with budget and behaved conservatively due to tail-energy inflation. High-budget KernelSHAP interaction references and larger d appear in the planned full-scale study but were not executed here [covert_2020_improving].

Experiment 2 (real tabular models). The Adult Income study, model training, masking engine, TreeSHAP references for XGBoost, and matched-budget drivers for KernelSHAP and SHAP-IQ were implemented but not executed in this pilot. No numerical results are reported.

Limitations of the executed runs. The heavy-hitter stage in the prototype used parity-based nomination rather than a full hash-and-peel sWHT, preserving the CS-SHAP formalism and uncertainty pipeline but potentially increasing constants, especially for very large d or higher-order interactions. Matched-budget baseline comparisons and the end-to-end real-model evaluations remain to be executed. Current evidence is qualitative and focused on behavior under controlled sparsity.

Fairness and hyperparameters. All reported CS-SHAP query counts include tail-energy probes. Anytime stopping and CI thresholds were held fixed within each pilot configuration. No baseline hyperparameters are involved since baselines were not run in this pilot. The provided protocols ensure future matched-budget and matched-link comparisons to KernelSHAP and SHAP-IQ with explicit query counting [covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap, muschalik_2024_shapiq].

Discussion and limitations. Beyond the need for full sWHT decoding, CS-SHAP assumes local compressibility; a non-sparsity detector mitigates wasted budget by falling back to sampling explainers when the spectrum is dense. The observational setting depends on the imputer quality [chau_2021_rkhs]. Finally, Shapley Residuals motivate reporting residual components, which our tail-energy term partially addresses but could be refined in future work [author_year_shapley].
paper_content.conclusion:
We presented Compressive SHAP, a black-box, gradient-free method that reframes local Shapley explanation as sparse recovery on the subset lattice. By directly estimating dominant Walsh coefficients with O(s·polylog d) masked queries and mapping them exactly to Shapley values via subset–Möbius transforms, CS-SHAP achieves sublinear query complexity under compressibility, recovers explicit interactions, and returns uncertainty-aware anytime outputs. The pipeline includes order-skewed heavy-hitter decoding, robust median-of-means amplitude estimation, tail-energy–based confidence intervals, and a non-sparsity test with fallback to sampling [covert_2020_improving, fumagalli_2023_shap, muschalik_2024_shapiq]. Pilot executions on synthetic games and interaction discovery demonstrate monotone error reduction with budget, accurate low-order recovery, conservative uncertainty, and benefits from order-skewed hashing.

CS-SHAP complements structure-specific explainers for trees and kernels [yu_2022_linear, chau_2021_rkhs], stabilized surrogates [tan_2023_glime], and uncertainty-aware sampling [slack_2020_reliable], and aligns with broader calls for efficient, reliable XAI in regulated contexts [chuang_2023_efficient, goodman_2016_european]. Future work includes: (i) implementing a full hash-and-peel sWHT to tighten constants and improve heavy-hitter recovery at scale; (ii) executing the complete experimental suite with matched-budget KernelSHAP and SHAP-IQ on large d and real models; (iii) strengthening CI calibration via multi-table bootstrap and refined tail-energy translation; (iv) expanding hierarchical grouping for high-resolution modalities; and (v) amortizing CS-SHAP as a teacher to train real-time explainers such as CoRTX with fewer labels [chuang_2023_cortx]. We also plan to connect residual diagnostics to recent analyses of Shapley limits and stabilized imaging pipelines [author_year_shapley, author_year_slice], advancing reliable, budget-aware explainability.

@article{blanc_2022_query,
 abstract = {We design an algorithm for finding counterfactuals with strong theoretical
guarantees on its performance. For any monotone model $f : X^d \to \{0,1\}$ and
instance $x^\star$, our algorithm makes \[ {S(f)^{O(\Delta_f(x^\star))}\cdot
\log d}\] queries to $f$ and returns {an {\sl optimal}} counterfactual for
$x^\star$: a nearest instance $x'$ to $x^\star$ for which $f(x')\ne
f(x^\star)$. Here $S(f)$ is the sensitivity of $f$, a discrete analogue of the
Lipschitz constant, and $\Delta_f(x^\star)$ is the distance from $x^\star$ to
its nearest counterfactuals. The previous best known query complexity was
$d^{\,O(\Delta_f(x^\star))}$, achievable by brute-force local search. We
further prove a lower bound of $S(f)^{\Omega(\Delta_f(x^\star))} + \Omega(\log
d)$ on the query complexity of any algorithm, thereby showing that the
guarantees of our algorithm are essentially optimal.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2207.07072v1.pdf},
 author = {Guy Blanc and Caleb Koch and Jane Lange and Li-Yang Tan},
 title = {A query-optimal algorithm for finding counterfactuals},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{chau_2021_rkhs,
 abstract = {Feature attribution for kernel methods is often heuristic and not
individualised for each prediction. To address this, we turn to the concept of
Shapley values~(SV), a coalition game theoretical framework that has previously
been applied to different machine learning model interpretation tasks, such as
linear models, tree ensembles and deep networks. By analysing SVs from a
functional perspective, we propose \textsc{RKHS-SHAP}, an attribution method
for kernel machines that can efficiently compute both \emph{Interventional} and
\emph{Observational Shapley values} using kernel mean embeddings of
distributions. We show theoretically that our method is robust with respect to
local perturbations - a key yet often overlooked desideratum for consistent
model interpretation. Further, we propose \emph{Shapley regulariser},
applicable to a general empirical risk minimisation framework, allowing
learning while controlling the level of specific feature's contributions to the
model. We demonstrate that the Shapley regulariser enables learning which is
robust to covariate shift of a given feature and fair learning which controls
the SVs of sensitive features.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2110.09167v2.pdf},
 author = {Siu Lun Chau and Robert Hu and Javier Gonzalez and Dino Sejdinovic},
 title = {RKHS-SHAP: Shapley Values for Kernel Methods},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{chuang_2023_cortx,
 abstract = {Recent advancements in explainable machine learning provide effective and
faithful solutions for interpreting model behaviors. However, many explanation
methods encounter efficiency issues, which largely limit their deployments in
practical scenarios. Real-time explainer (RTX) frameworks have thus been
proposed to accelerate the model explanation process by learning a
one-feed-forward explainer. Existing RTX frameworks typically build the
explainer under the supervised learning paradigm, which requires large amounts
of explanation labels as the ground truth. Considering that accurate
explanation labels are usually hard to obtain due to constrained computational
resources and limited human efforts, effective explainer training is still
challenging in practice. In this work, we propose a COntrastive Real-Time
eXplanation (CoRTX) framework to learn the explanation-oriented representation
and relieve the intensive dependence of explainer training on explanation
labels. Specifically, we design a synthetic strategy to select positive and
negative instances for the learning of explanation. Theoretical analysis show
that our selection strategy can benefit the contrastive learning process on
explanation tasks. Experimental results on three real-world datasets further
demonstrate the efficiency and efficacy of our proposed CoRTX framework.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.02794v1.pdf},
 author = {Yu-Neng Chuang and Guanchu Wang and Fan Yang and Quan Zhou and Pushkar Tripathi and Xuanting Cai and Xia Hu},
 github_url = {https://github.com/ynchuang/CoRTX-720},
 title = {CoRTX: Contrastive Framework for Real-time Explanation},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{fumagalli_2023_shap,
 abstract = {Predominately in explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) research, the
Shapley value (SV) is applied to determine feature attributions for any black
box model. Shapley interaction indices extend the SV to define any-order
feature interactions. Defining a unique Shapley interaction index is an open
research question and, so far, three definitions have been proposed, which
differ by their choice of axioms. Moreover, each definition requires a specific
approximation technique. Here, we propose SHAPley Interaction Quantification
(SHAP-IQ), an efficient sampling-based approximator to compute Shapley
interactions for arbitrary cardinal interaction indices (CII), i.e. interaction
indices that satisfy the linearity, symmetry and dummy axiom. SHAP-IQ is based
on a novel representation and, in contrast to existing methods, we provide
theoretical guarantees for its approximation quality, as well as estimates for
the variance of the point estimates. For the special case of SV, our approach
reveals a novel representation of the SV and corresponds to Unbiased KernelSHAP
with a greatly simplified calculation. We illustrate the computational
efficiency and effectiveness by explaining language, image classification and
high-dimensional synthetic models.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.01179v3.pdf},
 author = {Fabian Fumagalli and Maximilian Muschalik and Patrick Kolpaczki and Eyke Hüllermeier and Barbara Hammer},
 title = {SHAP-IQ: Unified Approximation of any-order Shapley Interactions},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{ley_2023_globe,
 abstract = {Counterfactual explanations have been widely studied in explainability, with
a range of application dependent methods prominent in fairness, recourse and
model understanding. The major shortcoming associated with these methods,
however, is their inability to provide explanations beyond the local or
instance-level. While many works touch upon the notion of a global explanation,
typically suggesting to aggregate masses of local explanations in the hope of
ascertaining global properties, few provide frameworks that are both reliable
and computationally tractable. Meanwhile, practitioners are requesting more
efficient and interactive explainability tools. We take this opportunity to
propose Global & Efficient Counterfactual Explanations (GLOBE-CE), a flexible
framework that tackles the reliability and scalability issues associated with
current state-of-the-art, particularly on higher dimensional datasets and in
the presence of continuous features. Furthermore, we provide a unique
mathematical analysis of categorical feature translations, utilising it in our
method. Experimental evaluation with publicly available datasets and user
studies demonstrate that GLOBE-CE performs significantly better than the
current state-of-the-art across multiple metrics (e.g., speed, reliability).},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2305.17021v2.pdf},
 author = {Dan Ley and Saumitra Mishra and Daniele Magazzeni},
 title = {GLOBE-CE: A Translation Based Approach for Global Counterfactual Explanations},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{muschalik_2024_shapiq,
 abstract = {Originally rooted in game theory, the Shapley Value (SV) has recently become
an important tool in machine learning research. Perhaps most notably, it is
used for feature attribution and data valuation in explainable artificial
intelligence. Shapley Interactions (SIs) naturally extend the SV and address
its limitations by assigning joint contributions to groups of entities, which
enhance understanding of black box machine learning models. Due to the
exponential complexity of computing SVs and SIs, various methods have been
proposed that exploit structural assumptions or yield probabilistic estimates
given limited resources. In this work, we introduce shapiq, an open-source
Python package that unifies state-of-the-art algorithms to efficiently compute
SVs and any-order SIs in an application-agnostic framework. Moreover, it
includes a benchmarking suite containing 11 machine learning applications of
SIs with pre-computed games and ground-truth values to systematically assess
computational performance across domains. For practitioners, shapiq is able to
explain and visualize any-order feature interactions in predictions of models,
including vision transformers, language models, as well as XGBoost and LightGBM
with TreeSHAP-IQ. With shapiq, we extend shap beyond feature attributions and
consolidate the application of SVs and SIs in machine learning that facilitates
future research. The source code and documentation are available at
https://github.com/mmschlk/shapiq.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2410.01649v1.pdf},
 author = {Maximilian Muschalik and Hubert Baniecki and Fabian Fumagalli and Patrick Kolpaczki and Barbara Hammer and Eyke Hüllermeier},
 github_url = {https://github.com/mmschlk/shapiq},
 title = {shapiq: Shapley Interactions for Machine Learning},
 year = {2024}
}

@article{parmentier_2021_optimal,
 abstract = {Counterfactual explanations are usually generated through heuristics that are
sensitive to the search's initial conditions. The absence of guarantees of
performance and robustness hinders trustworthiness. In this paper, we take a
disciplined approach towards counterfactual explanations for tree ensembles. We
advocate for a model-based search aiming at "optimal" explanations and propose
efficient mixed-integer programming approaches. We show that isolation forests
can be modeled within our framework to focus the search on plausible
explanations with a low outlier score. We provide comprehensive coverage of
additional constraints that model important objectives, heterogeneous data
types, structural constraints on the feature space, along with resource and
actionability restrictions. Our experimental analyses demonstrate that the
proposed search approach requires a computational effort that is orders of
magnitude smaller than previous mathematical programming algorithms. It scales
up to large data sets and tree ensembles, where it provides, within seconds,
systematic explanations grounded on well-defined models solved to optimality.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.06631v2.pdf},
 author = {Axel Parmentier and Thibaut Vidal},
 github_url = {https://github.com/vidalt/OCEAN},
 title = {Optimal Counterfactual Explanations in Tree Ensembles},
 year = {2021}
}

@article{slack_2020_reliable,
 abstract = {As black box explanations are increasingly being employed to establish model
credibility in high-stakes settings, it is important to ensure that these
explanations are accurate and reliable. However, prior work demonstrates that
explanations generated by state-of-the-art techniques are inconsistent,
unstable, and provide very little insight into their correctness and
reliability. In addition, these methods are also computationally inefficient,
and require significant hyper-parameter tuning. In this paper, we address the
aforementioned challenges by developing a novel Bayesian framework for
generating local explanations along with their associated uncertainty. We
instantiate this framework to obtain Bayesian versions of LIME and KernelSHAP
which output credible intervals for the feature importances, capturing the
associated uncertainty. The resulting explanations not only enable us to make
concrete inferences about their quality (e.g., there is a 95% chance that the
feature importance lies within the given range), but are also highly consistent
and stable. We carry out a detailed theoretical analysis that leverages the
aforementioned uncertainty to estimate how many perturbations to sample, and
how to sample for faster convergence. This work makes the first attempt at
addressing several critical issues with popular explanation methods in one
shot, thereby generating consistent, stable, and reliable explanations with
guarantees in a computationally efficient manner. Experimental evaluation with
multiple real world datasets and user studies demonstrate that the efficacy of
the proposed framework.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.05030v4.pdf},
 author = {Dylan Slack and Sophie Hilgard and Sameer Singh and Himabindu Lakkaraju},
 title = {Reliable Post hoc Explanations: Modeling Uncertainty in Explainability},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{tan_2023_glime,
 abstract = {As black-box machine learning models grow in complexity and find applications
in high-stakes scenarios, it is imperative to provide explanations for their
predictions. Although Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME)
[22] is a widely adpoted method for understanding model behaviors, it is
unstable with respect to random seeds [35,24,3] and exhibits low local fidelity
(i.e., how well the explanation approximates the model's local behaviors)
[21,16]. Our study shows that this instability problem stems from small sample
weights, leading to the dominance of regularization and slow convergence.
Additionally, LIME's sampling neighborhood is non-local and biased towards the
reference, resulting in poor local fidelity and sensitivity to reference
choice. To tackle these challenges, we introduce GLIME, an enhanced framework
extending LIME and unifying several prior methods. Within the GLIME framework,
we derive an equivalent formulation of LIME that achieves significantly faster
convergence and improved stability. By employing a local and unbiased sampling
distribution, GLIME generates explanations with higher local fidelity compared
to LIME. GLIME explanations are independent of reference choice. Moreover,
GLIME offers users the flexibility to choose a sampling distribution based on
their specific scenarios.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2311.15722v1.pdf},
 author = {Zeren Tan and Yang Tian and Jian Li},
 github_url = {https://github.com/thutzr/GLIME-General-Stable-and-Local-LIME-Explanation},
 title = {GLIME: General, Stable and Local LIME Explanation},
 year = {2023}
}

@article{wang_2022_accelerating,
 abstract = {Even though Shapley value provides an effective explanation for a DNN model
prediction, the computation relies on the enumeration of all possible input
feature coalitions, which leads to the exponentially growing complexity. To
address this problem, we propose a novel method SHEAR to significantly
accelerate the Shapley explanation for DNN models, where only a few coalitions
of input features are involved in the computation. The selection of the feature
coalitions follows our proposed Shapley chain rule to minimize the absolute
error from the ground-truth Shapley values, such that the computation can be
both efficient and accurate. To demonstrate the effectiveness, we
comprehensively evaluate SHEAR across multiple metrics including the absolute
error from the ground-truth Shapley value, the faithfulness of the
explanations, and running speed. The experimental results indicate SHEAR
consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baseline methods across different
evaluation metrics, which demonstrates its potentials in real-world
applications where the computational resource is limited.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2206.08529v2.pdf},
 author = {Guanchu Wang and Yu-Neng Chuang and Mengnan Du and Fan Yang and Quan Zhou and Pushkar Tripathi and Xuanting Cai and Xia Hu},
 title = {Accelerating Shapley Explanation via Contributive Cooperator Selection},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{wang_2022_accelerating,
 abstract = {Even though Shapley value provides an effective explanation for a DNN model
prediction, the computation relies on the enumeration of all possible input
feature coalitions, which leads to the exponentially growing complexity. To
address this problem, we propose a novel method SHEAR to significantly
accelerate the Shapley explanation for DNN models, where only a few coalitions
of input features are involved in the computation. The selection of the feature
coalitions follows our proposed Shapley chain rule to minimize the absolute
error from the ground-truth Shapley values, such that the computation can be
both efficient and accurate. To demonstrate the effectiveness, we
comprehensively evaluate SHEAR across multiple metrics including the absolute
error from the ground-truth Shapley value, the faithfulness of the
explanations, and running speed. The experimental results indicate SHEAR
consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baseline methods across different
evaluation metrics, which demonstrates its potentials in real-world
applications where the computational resource is limited.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2206.08529v2.pdf},
 author = {Guanchu Wang and Yu-Neng Chuang and Mengnan Du and Fan Yang and Quan Zhou and Pushkar Tripathi and Xuanting Cai and Xia Hu},
 title = {Accelerating Shapley Explanation via Contributive Cooperator Selection},
 year = {2022}
}

@article{woodruff_2020_near,
 abstract = {To accelerate kernel methods, we propose a near input sparsity time algorithm
for sampling the high-dimensional feature space implicitly defined by a kernel
transformation. Our main contribution is an importance sampling method for
subsampling the feature space of a degree $q$ tensoring of data points in
almost input sparsity time, improving the recent oblivious sketching method of
(Ahle et al., 2020) by a factor of $q^{5/2}/\epsilon^2$. This leads to a
subspace embedding for the polynomial kernel, as well as the Gaussian kernel,
with a target dimension that is only linearly dependent on the statistical
dimension of the kernel and in time which is only linearly dependent on the
sparsity of the input dataset. We show how our subspace embedding bounds imply
new statistical guarantees for kernel ridge regression. Furthermore, we
empirically show that in large-scale regression tasks, our algorithm
outperforms state-of-the-art kernel approximation methods.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2007.03927v2.pdf},
 author = {David P. Woodruff and Amir Zandieh},
 title = {Near Input Sparsity Time Kernel Embeddings via Adaptive Sampling},
 year = {2020}
}

@article{yu_2022_linear,
 abstract = {Decision trees are well-known due to their ease of interpretability. To
improve accuracy, we need to grow deep trees or ensembles of trees. These are
hard to interpret, offsetting their original benefits. Shapley values have
recently become a popular way to explain the predictions of tree-based machine
learning models. It provides a linear weighting to features independent of the
tree structure. The rise in popularity is mainly due to TreeShap, which solves
a general exponential complexity problem in polynomial time. Following
extensive adoption in the industry, more efficient algorithms are required.
This paper presents a more efficient and straightforward algorithm: Linear
TreeShap. Like TreeShap, Linear TreeShap is exact and requires the same amount
of memory.},
 arxiv_url = {https://arxiv.org/pdf/2209.08192v2.pdf},
 author = {Peng Yu and Chao Xu and Albert Bifet and Jesse Read},
 title = {Linear tree shap},
 year = {2022}
}
